one_line_takeaway: A 2024 survey that taxonomizes LLM data-privacy threats (passive leakage vs. active attacks) and maps defenses onto the pre-training / fine-tuning / inference stages, grounded in a hand-curated corpus of 91 papers rather than any new experiments.
key_finding: The survey's quantitative content is bibliometric, not experimental: from 91 collected papers it identifies 33 threat papers (5 leakage, 28 attacks) and 58 protection papers (11 pre-training, 23 fine-tuning, 24 inference), with 58.57% from 2023, 30 from 2021-2022, and 5 from 2024. It reports no attack-success or privacy-utility metrics of its own.
core_contribution: This is a survey (not an empirical paper) that organizes the literature on data-privacy risks and defenses for large language models. Its own contribution is a two-part taxonomy: (1) privacy THREATS split into passive "privacy leakage" (sensitive queries, contextual leakage, personal-preference inference) and active "privacy attacks" (backdoor/data-poisoning, membership inference, model inversion/data reconstruction, attribute inference, model stealing); and (2) privacy PROTECTION methods organized by the three LLM development stages — pre-training (data cleaning, federated learning, differential privacy), fine-tuning (federated learning, differential privacy, knowledge unlearning, offsite tuning), and inference (cryptography-based: homomorphic encryption, multi-party computation, functional secret sharing, DP; detection-based; and hardware-based: data locality, confidential computing/TEEs). It then maps threats to defenses and lays out open challenges (black-box interpretability, multimodal LLMs, personalized LLMs, whole-lifecycle protection, hardware-assisted protection). The stage-based protection taxonomy is the paper's own framing; the individual techniques and attacks it describes are all drawn from cited prior work.
threat_model: The survey frames two threat classes against LLMs. Passive privacy leakage: sensitive information reaching or being exposed by the model without a deliberate adversary — users typing PII into prompts (e.g., the cited Samsung/ChatGPT incident), contextual leakage from otherwise-innocuous queries, and inference of personal preferences; also training-data memorization surfacing at inference. Active privacy attacks: an adversary deliberately extracting private data, covering backdoor/data-poisoning attacks (on pre-training and fine-tuning), membership inference attacks (pre-training and fine-tuning), model inversion / data reconstruction, attribute inference, and model stealing. Adversary capabilities range from black-box query access (e.g., prompting GPT-3.5-turbo to "diverge" and emit training data) to poisoning training/fine-tuning data. This is a synthesized threat taxonomy over the literature, not a threat model instantiated for a specific system.
limitations: As a survey it contributes no new algorithm, dataset, or empirical evaluation, and it does not quantitatively compare the surveyed defenses (no unified benchmark of privacy-utility tradeoffs). The corpus is modest (91 papers via a Google Scholar search) and the field moves fast, so coverage is a 2024 snapshot skewed toward 2023 publications. The authors themselves note that privacy research concentrates on older, smaller open models (GPT-2, BERT) while larger and closed recent LLMs remain under-scrutinized, and that multimodal and personalized LLM privacy are largely unaddressed. Reported statistics depend on the authors' manual categorization and search terms, so the counts are indicative rather than exhaustive.

quantitative_results: 91 papers collected via Google Scholar search
33 papers on privacy threats: 5 on privacy leakage, 28 on privacy attacks
58 papers on privacy protection: 11 pre-training, 23 fine-tuning, 24 inference
No experimental/quantitative results of the authors' own (survey reports no accuracy, attack-success, or utility metrics)
